title: Tarte tatin
subtitle: Upside-down caramelised apple tart under puff pastry
excerpt: Apples are cooked in butter and sugar directly in an oven-safe pan until deeply caramelised, then covered with a disc of puff pastry and baked until crisp. The tart is turned out immediately on leaving the oven, so the pastry becomes the base and the sticky caramelised apples the top. Cox or Braeburn hold their shape; Bramley collapses to purée.
sourceNotes: Tarte tatin is associated with the Hotel Tatin in Lamotte-Beuvron, Sologne, France, run by sisters Stéphanie and Caroline Tatin in the 1880s and 1890s. Whether the recipe arose from a mistake or deliberate invention is disputed. It appears in the French pâtisserie repertoire from the late 19th century and was popularised in Paris at Maxim's restaurant. The tart reached Britain through French restaurant culture and became widely known through the 1980s and 1990s. The recipe uses eating apples (Cox, Braeburn) that hold their shape during caramelisation, not cooking apples that dissolve.

content.text: The recipe is mostly stovetop work; the oven only crisps the pastry. The

is the step that demands full attention: turn the tart immediately when it leaves the oven, not ten minutes later. Have the plate ready and positioned before you open the oven door. Use oven gloves on both hands. The caramel is liquid and very hot when the tart first comes out.

attrs.prepNote: Cox or Braeburn, peeled, halved, and cored

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Put the butter and sugar in a 24 to 26 cm oven-safe frying pan over medium heat. Let the butter melt without stirring, then cook until the mixture turns deep amber. Add the vanilla. Remove from the heat and arrange the apple halves cut-side up in the caramel, packing them tightly as they will shrink during cooking. Return to the heat and cook for 15 to 20 minutes, turning the apples once, until softened and the caramel is deeply coloured.

Remove from the heat. Roll or cut the puff pastry to a circle slightly larger than the pan. Lay the pastry over the apples and tuck the edges down inside the rim of the pan so the apples are fully enclosed. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and crisp. Remove from the oven. Place a large serving plate over the pan immediately. Flip in one confident motion, count to five, then lift the pan. Serve warm with crème fraîche or double cream.
